text:
Admission Date:  2007-11-27              Discharge Date:   2010-04-01

Date of Birth:  1987-06-18             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Daniel
Chief Complaint:
Trauma

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
2007-11-27 Gelfoam embolization was performed to the right hepatic
artery


History of Present Illness:
35M hit who fell from his horse and in the process hit [**Initials (NamePattern4) **] Hernandez
tree branches at around 4 PM on 07-18.  He was initially treated
at Pacific Orange Hospital, Llc Hospital with a Hct of 41 but on trauma eval was
found to have active extravasation from a branch of a hepatic
artery from segment II of the liver and concurrently had an
episode of hypotension.  He was transferred to Arrowhead Behavioral Health for further
management.  In the ED, he had an Hct of 33 and was transfused 2
units of PRBCs due to borderline hypotension.

INJURIES:
Segment III liver laceration with active blush


Past Medical History:
PMH: none

PSH: appendectomy

Social History:
smokes one pack daily. drinks six beers daily.

Family History:
non-contributory

Physical Exam:
Upon presentation to Arrowhead Behavioral Health:
Temp: 97.9 HR: 72 BP: 124/75 Resp: 22 O(2)Sat: 100 RA
Normal

Constitutional: pale, ill-appearing
HEENT: pupils 12-31 bilaterally, superficial lacerations on
forehead, Normocephalic, atraumatic, Pupils equal, round and
reactive to light, Extraocular muscles intact
Oropharynx within normal limits
Chest: Breath sounds reproducable bilaterally
Cardiovascular: Regular Rate and Rhythm
Abdominal: diffusely tender, firm, no guarding, no rebound,
small abrasion on L superior abdomen, Nondistended
Pelvic: pelvis stable
GU/Flank: no perineal tenderness , No costovertebral angle
tenderness
Extr/Back: Palpable distal pulses, no gross deformities on
BUE or BLE., No cyanosis, clubbing or edema
Skin: No rash, Warm and dry
Neuro: Neuro exam normal , Speech fluent
Psych: Normal mood, Normal mentation
Heme/Tyler/Tyler: No petechiae

07-18 CT Torso: Liver laceration through left lobe (segment III)
with adjacent active extravasation. Appears to be injury to an
arterial branch supplying the left lobe. Main hepatic artery at
hilum is intact.  Moderate hemorrhagic free fluid surrounding
the liver, spleen and in the midpelvis. No acute fractures. No
other traumatic pathology within the chest, abdomen or pelvis.
07-18 CT C-spine: No acute fracture, malalignment or
prevertebral soft tissue swelling. Loss of the usual cervical
lordosis -straightened c-spine. Mild rightward scoliosis.
Moderate degenerative changes most severe at C5-C6.

Brief Hospital Course:
His ICU course as follows as dictated by PGY2 surgery resident:
07-18: IR: angiogram, right hepatic artery angiogram -- no
active extrav --> performed prophylactic gel foam injection to
right lobe of liver; admitted to TSICU, post-procedure stable
06-23: Hct stable. Diet advanced and tolerated. PCA changed to
Morphine with good results.  started hydralazine for SBP>170s.
11-12: start Norvasc 5mg PO daily. hematocrit and hemodynamics
stable, transferred to the floor

His floor course as followed:
Once transferred to the floor he continued to progress. His
hematocrits were followed closely and remained stable.
Hemodynamically his vital signs were also stable - although
despite the Norvasc his blood pressures remained slightly
elevated. Upon further discussion with patient he reported that
he had previously been prescribed Lisinopril 5 mg daily by his
PCP but stopped taking it some months ago when the prescription
ran out. It was discussed with patient that he would be given a
new prescription for this with refills upon discharge but that
he should follow up with his primary care doctor within the next
couple of weeks.

His pain medications were changed to oral form and his pain
control was adequate with this regimen.

He is being discharged to home on HD#3 and will follow up in
Acute Care Clinic in 03-20 weeks and with his PCP for ongoing
management of his blood pressure.

Medications on Admission:
none

Discharge Medications:
1. acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every
6 hours) as needed for pain.
2. senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO BID (2 times a day)
as needed for Constipation.
3. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day) as needed for constipation.
4. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO every 4-6 hours as
needed for pain.
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
5. Lisinopril 5 mg 1 tab po daily

Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
s/p Fall off horse
Liver laceration through left lobe (segment III)
Acute blood loss anemia

Secondary diagnosis:
Hypertension

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital after a trauma where you
sustained injury to your liver requiring that you undergo a
procedure to stop the bleeding in the ateries within the liver.
You were monitored closely in the intensive care unit where your
blood counts were followed to insure that there was no further
bleeding. As your blood counts stabilized you were transferredto
the regular surgical unit.

GO TO THE NEAREST EMERGENCY ROOM IMMEDIATELY IF YOU SUDDENLY
BECOME DIZZY, LIGHTHEADED AS IF GOING TO PASS OUT. THESE CAN BE
SIGNS OF INTERNAL BLEDING AND WILL REQUIRE PROMPT EVALUATION.

AVOID any contact sports of any kind for the next 8 weeks as
this can cause re-injury and bleeding to your liver.

You may resume your usual home medications as prescribed.

If you are taking narcotics for pain control do not take other
narcotics along with these, avoid alcohol and/or other illicit
drugs while taking these medications. It is also advisable that
you do not drive while taking narcotics.

Followup Instructions:
Department: GENERAL SURGERY/Wyoming Behavioral Health
When: TUESDAY 1997-01-17 at 2:00 PM
With: ACUTE CARE CLINIC 577-366-8915
Building: LM Cardiology Bldg (Harper) Unit 9632 Box 7884
DPO AE 98311
Campus: WEST     Best Parking: Cardiology Garage

Follow up with your primary care doctor in the next 2-3 weeks
for a general physical. You will need to call for an
appointment.
